market_notes: STABILIZING: SPY 1W -0.2%, QQQ 1W -0.6%, SMH 1W -1.8% — meaningful improvement from late March lows. Iran war-exit signals reducing geopolitical risk premium; crude retreating second consecutive session. AI capex cycle INTACT: NVDA ecosystem investments accelerating (Lumentum, Synopsys, Nokia, XAI, OpenAI deals in March alone), OpenAI $122B at $852B, JPM $3.8B AI data center bond. Portfolio +4.3% total P&L — outperforming SPY (-3.4% 1M), QQQ (-2.8% 1M), SMH (+0.2% 1M) over 1-month period. All 13 positions above stops. MRVL at +12.5% unrealized is the standout — near 52W highs post-NVDA partnership. Cash at ~5% ($5,162) limits new additions without trimming. Earnings season beginning: JPM/GS/C/MS reporting April 13-15 key macro read-through. Semiconductor tariff carveouts still pending — asymmetric positive catalyst for AVGO, NVDA, ARM, MRVL.
watching: JPM/GS/C/MS earnings April 13-15 — financial sector macro health read-through; watch for loan loss provisions, investment banking activity, AI lending themes
APLD earnings April 8 — AI infrastructure demand signal from AI data center operator
ASML earnings April 15 — leading indicator for semiconductor capex cycle health; guidance critical
LNG — Iran peace deal progress; at -7.1% from cost and $261.28 stop; Qatar LNG shock analysis supportive of structural thesis; trim if recovers to cost and Iran deal confirmed
CVX — at -6.5% from cost; oil retreating on Iran signals; FCF remains robust at $70/bbl; stop $185.81 provides protection; hold unless macro deteriorates

MRVL — at +12.5% unrealized, near 52W high; Bank of America raised PT post-NVDA NVLink partnership; next catalyst Q1 earnings and hyperscaler ASIC win announcements; consider trimming 20-30% if it exceeds $115-120 to raise cash for PANW or TSM
PANW — CEO bought $10M personally per Cramer; +4.9% 1W, +2.9% 1M; cybersecurity secular theme; would initiate 3-4% position on trim of MRVL or energy names
META — +10.2% unrealized at $579; Q2 earnings key for ad revenue validation and AI ROI; safety concerns article (Barrons) worth monitoring but not thesis-breaking
ARM — IBD Composite Rating 96; Cramer top semiconductor pick; AGI chip commercialization timeline is key catalyst; stop $122.51 provides good buffer at $155
Semiconductor tariff carveouts — pending positive catalyst for AVGO, NVDA, ARM, MRVL, TSM
TSM — $341.49, fwd P/E 19x, 45% margins, 35% ROE, -12.2% from 52W high; high conviction add target if cash becomes available; world's irreplaceable semiconductor manufacturer
Cash management — at 5% ($5,162); cannot add without trimming; primary candidates for partial trim if thesis weakens: LNG (energy risk premium fading), CVX (same), or MRVL on extended strength

recent_runs.reasoning: This is day one with all positions just entered at cost (0% P&L), so the immediate priority is to assess whether the initial portfolio construction makes sense and address the anomalous META short position. The mandate explicitly states LONG ONLY — no short selling — so the META short must be closed immediately. Beyond that, the portfolio is well-positioned in AI infrastructure names (NVDA, MSFT, GOOGL, AMZN, AVGO, ARM, MRVL, EQIX) at reasonable valuations following macro-driven selloffs. Cash stands at ~$15.7K (~15.7% of portfolio) which is appropriate given elevated macro uncertainty around tariffs and growth concerns.

The portfolio is well-constructed in AI infrastructure with 8 core positions at cost (day one entries). The macro environment remains challenging with tariff uncertainty and growth concerns driving broad market weakness, but AI capex intentions from hyperscalers remain unchanged. I'm observing notable relative strength in energy names (CVX +14.7% 1M, XOM +15.1% 1M, LNG +27.7% 1M, EQT +13.1% 1M, HAL +13.7% 1M) and selective tech names (MRVL +19.7%, DELL +41.5%, CIEN +17.7%, NET +16.2%) while the core AI semiconductor complex consolidates. With $20.6K cash (~20.6% of portfolio) and only 8 of 30 permitted positions filled, there is capacity to add diversification across energy infrastructure and additional AI plays at attractive valuations.

Portfolio is newly constructed with 12 positions and $5,162 cash (~5.2% of portfolio). All positions were just entered at cost so there is no P&L to manage. The macro environment remains challenging: Iran tensions are boosting energy names (LNG, CVX showing continued strength), while AI semiconductor complex consolidates on tariff uncertainty. The Swing Trader is SHORT META at the same entry price as my long — this is a meaningful near-term momentum signal worth noting. Key observations: (1) META faces new legal headwinds (social media addiction lawsuit loss in LA) and the swing trader shorting it signals bearish near-term momentum; however my long-term thesis on Meta's advertising moat and AI ROI remains intact — I'll hold but watch closely. (2) Energy sector momentum continues — PSX +23.5% 1M, XOM +15.1% 1M, EQT +13.1% 1M, HAL +13.7% 1M suggest the Iran/geopolitical premium is not yet fading. (3) With only $5,162 cash remaining (~5.2%), I'm near the 3% floor and cannot deploy meaningfully without selling something. (4) CIEN had FTSE inclusion catalyst and +17.7% 1M — thesis intact. (5) ARM showing strong relative strength at +11.5% 1M and +8.9% 1W — SoftBank deepening OpenAI/ARM bet is a positive signal. (6) I have 12 positions vs 30 max — room exists but cash is limiting. The prudent action is to hold all current positions, let the new entries develop, and wait for either cash generation (from wins) or better entry points rather than chasing names at extended valuations. Cash at ~5.2% is tight but above the 3% floor.

All 12 positions were entered at cost just yesterday (2026-03-29), so there is no P&L to manage and no thesis has been tested yet. The macro environment remains challenging with tariff uncertainty, Iran conflict premium in energy, and AI semiconductor complex under pressure. With only $5,162 cash (~5.2% of portfolio) I am near the 3% floor and cannot add meaningful new positions without trimming existing ones. Key observations: (1) META and GOOGL both lost a major social media addiction lawsuit in LA — this is a meaningful legal headwind but doesn't break the long-term advertising moat thesis; holding both. (2) The swing trader remains SHORT META — near-term bearish momentum signal noted but thesis intact. (3) DELL +41.5% 1M, TSEM +38.8% 1M are showing explosive momentum but I'm already at near-full deployment. (4) ARM showing continued relative strength at +8.9% 1W — SoftBank/OpenAI thesis playing out. (5) Energy names (LNG +5.7% 1W, CVX +4.7% 1W, HAL +10.7% 1W) continuing to outperform as Australian LNG outage and Iran tensions persist. (6) No positions are at stop-loss levels. The correct action is patient holding — all positions are day-old entries at cost with intact theses, and insufficient cash to add new positions without degrading existing high-conviction holdings.

The portfolio is fully deployed with 12 positions and only ~$5,162 cash (~5.2%). The market remains in a risk-off environment with SPY -2.9% 1W and QQQ -4.2% 1W. My positions are largely flat to slightly negative since entry (0-5 days ago), with notable underperformance in CIEN (-5.4%), MRVL (-3.2%), and AVGO (-1.2%). The AI infrastructure thesis remains intact with hyperscaler capex unchanged, Iran conflict supporting energy names, and ARM/LNG/CVX showing relative strength. No positions have violated stop losses. Two headlines call out 'Magnificent 7 stunning plunges' — AMZN, GOOGL, META, MSFT, NVDA appear to be referenced — suggesting continued near-term pressure but not thesis-breaking. CIEN is approaching its stop zone (-15% from entry requires another -10% decline) but the FTSE inclusion and optical demand thesis remain intact. Morgan Stanley named META their new top pick following selloff — validating my long thesis despite swing trader's short position. With only $5,162 cash available and all positions still young (2-3 days), patience is the correct posture. No stop losses triggered, all theses intact, and insufficient cash to add meaningfully without degrading existing positions.

The market environment has materially deteriorated since portfolio construction — SPY -3.6% 1W, QQQ -5.1% 1W, SMH -7.4% 1W, with oil surging above $100 on Iran conflict escalation and the VIX up 50% in a month per headlines. Several key positions are showing significant unrealized losses: MRVL -7.5% (approaching stop at $83.49), CIEN -9.1% (stop at $341.37 still ~6.6% away), ARM -5.0%, AVGO -2.4%. The core AI semiconductor thesis faces multiple headwinds: tariff uncertainty on chips, Trump/Powell comments roiling Nasdaq, Micron diving. Meanwhile energy (CVX +2.7% 1W, LNG +2.2% 1W, XOM +6.4% 1W) and defensives (COST +3.2% 1W, EQIX -0.3% 1W) are providing relative strength. With only $5,162 cash (~5.2%) and 12 positions, the primary decisions are whether any stop losses should be proactively managed and whether MRVL's continued weakness warrants preemptive action. MRVL at $87.81 is now just 5.2% above its stop ($83.49) — with the stock down -7.5% from cost and the semiconductor sector under acute pressure (MRVL -2.6% 1W), I need to weigh whether to hold through volatility or protect capital. The stop exists for this reason — I will maintain it but not act prematurely. No earnings alerts for held positions this session. The correct posture remains patient holding with stops intact.

**Macro:** The market environment has shifted materially positive today — the Dow surged 1,100+ points on Trump signaling Iran war exit 'in two to three weeks,' crude oil retreating from multiyear highs, and semiconductor/AI stocks rallying sharply. MRVL surged on NVIDIA's $2B investment/partnership announcement, ARM surged on its AGI chip news, and the risk-off pressure that dominated the past several weeks is materially easing. SPY 1W -0.4%, QQQ 1W -1.2%, SMH 1W -2.8% — all significantly improved from last session's SMH -7.4% 1W. The AI capex cycle remains intact with OpenAI closing a $122B funding round at $852B valuation and JPMorgan's $3.8B AI data center bond drawing $14B in orders. **Sectors:** AI infrastructure (semiconductors, data centers, networking) is the strongest multi-year theme — today's NVDA $2B MRVL investment and ARM AGI chip developments reinforce the thesis. Energy is giving back some gains as Iran war-end signals emerge (crude retreating), but LNG structural thesis on European demand remains intact. Cybersecurity (PANW +6.8% 1M) and defensive consumer (COST, TJX) remain solid diversifiers. **Positions:** Portfolio is showing strong recovery today — MRVL +7.2% 1W (after being critically close to stop), ARM +12.1% 1W, META +8.8% unrealized P&L at $572. All 13 positions are above stops. The MRVL situation has resolved dramatically — NVIDIA's $2B investment validates the custom silicon thesis completely. No stop losses are under immediate threat. With only $5,162 cash (~5.2%), I cannot add meaningfully without trimming. The total portfolio is up +2.8% ($2,766) — improved significantly. Key question: does today's rally create any trim opportunities or add opportunities? LNG is down -4.4% from cost and -3.7% 1W as Iran war-end reduces energy risk premium, which is worth monitoring. CVX -2.0% from cost but structurally fine. No actions are warranted — the portfolio is well-positioned, all theses are intact or strengthened, and cash is near the floor. Patience remains the correct posture.

**Macro:** The market is showing tentative stabilization after Q1 turbulence — SPY -0.2% 1W, QQQ -0.6% 1W, SMH -1.8% 1W, all improved from the deep selloffs of late March. Iran war-exit signals continue to reduce geopolitical risk premium, crude retreating further, and the AI capex cycle remains structurally intact with OpenAI's $122B raise at $852B valuation, NVIDIA's continued ecosystem investment ($2B in MRVL, Lumentum, Synopsys, Nokia deals per news), and JPM's $3.8B AI data center bond that drew $14B in orders. Semiconductor tariff carveouts remain pending but possible — a positive asymmetric catalyst. Earnings season begins this week with financials (JPM, GS, C, MS on April 13-15) providing a macro health read-through. **Sectors:** AI infrastructure (semiconductors, data centers, networking) remains the dominant multi-year theme — MRVL +37.7% 1M, CIEN +24.7% 1M, ARM +27.4% 1M showing exceptional relative strength even in a choppy tape. Energy is giving back Iran risk premium but LNG's structural European demand thesis remains intact (Qatar LNG shock analysis positive for Cheniere per recent coverage). Cybersecurity (PANW +4.9% 1W, CRWD +1.9% 1W) and defensive consumer (COST +2.2% 1W, TJX +1.4% 1W) continuing to hold up well. **Positions:** Portfolio total P&L now +4.3% ($4,303) — strong outperformance vs benchmarks (SPY -3.4% 1M, QQQ -2.8% 1M). All 13 positions are above their stops. Key observations: (1) MRVL is the star at +12.5% unrealized — Bank of America raised PT on NVDA partnership validation; at -1.1% from 52W high, it is near all-time highs with the thesis fully intact. (2) META +10.2% unrealized at $579 — strong performer, swing trader alignment (both long). (3) ARM +7.6% at $155 — Composite Rating rose to 96 per IBD, Jim Cramer calling it 'one of the most important companies in semiconductors.' (4) LNG -7.1% from cost at $275.84 — this is the weakest position with Iran peace deal signals reducing risk premium, but Qatar LNG shock analysis (Simply Wall St.) supports structural thesis; stop at $261.28 remains ~$14 below current price. (5) CVX -6.5% from cost — energy retreating but FCF generation remains strong at current oil prices. (6) CIEN -5.1% 1W this week but +3.4% unrealized; WaveLogic 6 deployment with Vodafone Idea is a positive catalyst. Cash at $5,162 (~5%) limits new additions without trimming. No stops are under immediate threat. The portfolio is well-constructed with strong conviction across all positions. No trades warranted this session — patience remains the correct posture as earnings season approaches.
